description:
You are a performance-engineer who just received two raw latency captures in CSV format.  
Your goal is to aggregate those captures and publish a machine-readable metrics report so that an automated build job can import the numbers.

STARTING FILES (already present):
1. /home/user/data/raw_run1.csv  
2. /home/user/data/raw_run2.csv  

Exact contents of the two CSV files:

/home/user/data/raw_run1.csv  
(timestamp,endpoint,latency_ms)
2023-09-01T10:00:00Z,/api/login,120
2023-09-01T10:00:01Z,/api/items,200
2023-09-01T10:00:02Z,/api/login,110
2023-09-01T10:00:03Z,/api/items,220
2023-09-01T10:00:04Z,/api/items,180

/home/user/data/raw_run2.csv  
(timestamp,endpoint,latency_ms)
2023-09-01T11:00:00Z,/api/login,130
2023-09-01T11:00:01Z,/api/items,210
2023-09-01T11:00:02Z,/api/login,140
2023-09-01T11:00:03Z,/api/items,190
2023-09-01T11:00:04Z,/api/login,150

TASK STEPS (high level):
1. Parse both CSV files concurrently/independently (i.e. do not simply `cat` them together first).  
2. Compute, for every distinct “endpoint”, two metrics across all rows of both files:  
   a. average_ms  – arithmetic mean of the latency_ms column, rounded to the nearest integer.  
   b. p95_ms      – 95th-percentile latency using the “nearest-rank” method  
      (position = ceil(0.95 * N) after ordering latencies for that endpoint ascending).  
3. Create directory /home/user/results (if it does not yet exist).  
4. Write the aggregated metrics to /home/user/results/latency_summary.json with the EXACT format given below.  
5. Produce an audit log called /home/user/results/merge_log.txt that the CI system can grep later.

REQUIRED FORMAT OF latency_summary.json
• The file must be valid UTF-8 JSON.  
• No root object other than the one shown below may be present; property order must match.  
• The file MUST contain ONLY the following structure and literal formatting (whitespace is insignificant):  

{
  "generated_at": "2023-09-02T00:00:00Z",
  "endpoints": [
    {
      "name": "/api/login",
      "metrics": {
        "average_ms": 130,
        "p95_ms": 150
      }
    },
    {
      "name": "/api/items",
      "metrics": {
        "average_ms": 200,
        "p95_ms": 220
      }
    }
  ]
}

REQUIRED FORMAT OF merge_log.txt
• Plain text.  
• Exactly two lines, one for each input file, **in any order**.  
• Line pattern: processed: <absolute_path_to_file>  
  Example (path must be absolute!):  
  processed: /home/user/data/raw_run1.csv

The automated test will verify:

1. /home/user/results/latency_summary.json exists, is valid JSON, and matches byte-for-byte the structure & numbers above (ignoring insignificant whitespace).  
2. /home/user/results/merge_log.txt exists, has exactly two non-empty lines that each start with “processed: ” followed by the absolute path of one of the two CSV files.  
3. No other files are modified in /home/user/data.  

Hints you might consider (NOT mandatory):  
• `awk -F,`, `csvtool`, `jq`, and GNU coreutils can all perform the required calculations.  
• Parallelisation can be simulated by reading the two input files in subshells or background jobs and then combining the intermediate results.

Remember: do NOT hard-code the full path of your own home directory into the JSON or log outputs except where the specification explicitly requires it. The only hard-coded time stamp allowed is the one in the JSON example above.
truth:
EXPECTED CONTENTS OF /home/user/results/latency_summary.json (after successful completion):

{"generated_at":"2023-09-02T00:00:00Z","endpoints":[{"name":"/api/login","metrics":{"average_ms":130,"p95_ms":150}},{"name":"/api/items","metrics":{"average_ms":200,"p95_ms":220}}]}

(Note: the JSON verifier will normalise insignificant whitespace; the exact byte sequence above is provided for clarity.)

EXPECTED CONTENTS OF /home/user/results/merge_log.txt:

processed: /home/user/data/raw_run1.csv
processed: /home/user/data/raw_run2.csv

No trailing blank lines are permitted.

Any deviation from these contents will cause the automated test to fail.
